claim_text: We assume a vanishing cosmological constant Λ = 0.
raw_response: {"claim_id":2,"verdict":"INCOMPLETE","explanation":"Standard Schwarzschild derivation sets Λ=0, but not required. With nonzero Λ, vacuum solution becomes Schwarzschild–de Sitter and horizon structure changes.", "confidence":0.97}

verdict.explanation: Standard Schwarzschild derivation sets Λ=0, but not required. With nonzero Λ, vacuum solution becomes Schwarzschild–de Sitter and horizon structure changes.

claim_text: We seek a solution in vacuum (T_{μν} = 0) exterior to a spherically symmetric, nonrotating, uncharged mass M.

verdict.explanation: Standard vacuum assumption for exterior Schwarzschild solution; appropriate for a spherically symmetric, nonrotating, uncharged mass.

claim_text: Vacuum implies R_{μν} = 0 (tracing the equations yields R = 0, so the Einstein tensor vanishes if and only if the Ricci tensor does).
raw_response: {"claim_id":4,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"In vacuum with zero cosmological constant, Einstein equations give G_{μν}=0. Tracing yields -R=0 in 4D, hence R_{μν}=G_{μν}+(1/2)Rg_{μν}=0, and conversely.","confidence":0.98}

verdict.explanation: In vacuum with zero cosmological constant, Einstein equations give G_{μν}=0. Tracing yields -R=0 in 4D, hence R_{μν}=G_{μν}+(1/2)Rg_{μν}=0, and conversely.

claim_text: Spherical symmetry further restricts the form of the line element to depend only on a radial coordinate r, with the angular sector reducing to the standard round 2-sphere.
raw_response: {"claim_id":6,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"Spherical symmetry implies SO(3) orbits are 2-spheres, so metric components depend only on coordinates orthogonal to orbits; choosing areal radius gives angular part r^2 dΩ^2.","confidence":0.97}

verdict.explanation: Spherical symmetry implies SO(3) orbits are 2-spheres, so metric components depend only on coordinates orthogonal to orbits; choosing areal radius gives angular part r^2 dΩ^2.

claim_text: Asymptotic flatness (Minkowski spacetime recovered as r → ∞) is also assumed.

verdict.explanation: Asymptotic flatness is a standard boundary condition in Schwarzschild derivations, ensuring Minkowski spacetime at infinity.

claim_text: The general ansatz is ds² = -A(r) c² dt² + B(r) dr² + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²), where A(r) → 1 and B(r) → 1 as r → ∞.
raw_response: {"claim_id":8,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"This is the standard static, spherically symmetric metric in areal radius gauge; asymptotic flatness requires A,B→1 as r→∞ for vacuum around isolated mass.","confidence":0.98}

verdict.explanation: This is the standard static, spherically symmetric metric in areal radius gauge; asymptotic flatness requires A,B→1 as r→∞ for vacuum around isolated mass.

claim_text: The nonvanishing Christoffel symbols for this metric are Γ^t_{tr} = A'/(2A), Γ^r_{tt} = c²A'/(2B), Γ^r_{rr} = B'/(2B), Γ^r_{θθ} = -r/B, Γ^r_{φφ} = -r sin²θ/B, Γ^θ_{rθ} = 1/r, Γ^φ_{rφ} = 1/r, Γ^φ_{θφ} = cotθ, Γ^θ_{φφ} = -sinθ cosθ.

verdict.explanation: Each Christoffel symbol correctly follows from the metric ds² = -A(r)c²dt² + B(r)dr² + r²dΩ² and its derivatives.

claim_text: The Einstein equations reduce to the independent conditions R_{tt} = 0, R_{rr} = 0, and R_{θθ} = 0.

verdict.explanation: Spherical symmetry and the vacuum condition imply that only R_tt, R_rr, and R_θθ are independent (with R_φφ redundant).

claim_text: The φφ component is proportional to the θθ component by spherical symmetry.
raw_response: {"claim_id":12,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"In spherical symmetry, angular metric components satisfy g_{\\phi\\phi}=g_{\\theta\\theta}\\sin^2\\theta, so Einstein tensor equations give G_{\\phi\\phi}=\\sin^2\\theta\\,G_{\\theta\\theta}.","confidence":0.98}

verdict.explanation: In spherical symmetry, angular metric components satisfy g_{\phi\phi}=g_{\theta\theta}\sin^2\theta, so Einstein tensor equations give G_{\phi\phi}=\sin^2\theta\,G_{\theta\theta}.

claim_text: Off-diagonal components such as R_{tr} vanish identically by staticity.

verdict.explanation: Staticity (and time-reversal symmetry) mandates a diagonal metric, so mixed R_tr components vanish.

claim_text: First consider R_{tr} = 0. Substituting the Christoffel symbols yields ∂_r(A'/A + B'/B) = 0.
raw_response: {"claim_id":14,"verdict":"ERROR","explanation":"For static spherical metric, \(R_{tr}\) vanishes identically because time derivatives are zero; it does not imply \u2202_r(A'/A+B'/B)=0.","confidence":0.98}

claim_text: Integration gives A(r)B(r) = K (constant).

verdict.explanation: The standard derivation yields d/dr ln[A(r)B(r)] = 0, so A(r)B(r) equals a constant, validating the claim.

claim_text: Asymptotic flatness forces K = 1, so B(r) = 1/A(r).
raw_response: {"claim_id":16,"verdict":"INCOMPLETE","explanation":"Vacuum Einstein equations imply A(r)B(r)=const. Asymptotic flatness sets const=1, giving B=1/A. Asymptotic flatness alone does not directly force this relation.","confidence":0.97}

verdict.explanation: Vacuum Einstein equations imply A(r)B(r)=const. Asymptotic flatness sets const=1, giving B=1/A. Asymptotic flatness alone does not directly force this relation.

claim_text: The metric simplifies to ds² = -A(r) c² dt² + dr²/A(r) + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).

verdict.explanation: It's the standard static, spherically symmetric metric ansatz leading to the Schwarzschild solution.

raw_response: {"claim_id":18,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"For ds²=-A(r)dt²+B(r)dr²+r²dΩ² with (-,+,+,+) signature, direct computation gives R_{θθ}=1-1/B-rA'/(2AB)+rB'/(2B²).","confidence":0.98}

verdict.explanation: For ds²=-A(r)dt²+B(r)dr²+r²dΩ² with (-,+,+,+) signature, direct computation gives R_{θθ}=1-1/B-rA'/(2AB)+rB'/(2B²).

claim_text: Setting R_{θθ} = 0 with B = 1/A (so B' = -A'/A²) yields 1 - A - rA' = 0.

verdict.explanation: Substituting B=1/A and its derivative into R_θθ indeed correctly gives 1 - A - rA' = 0.

claim_text: The equation 1 - A - rA' = 0 is equivalent to dA/(1-A) = dr/r.
raw_response: {"claim_id":20,"verdict":"ERROR","explanation":"From 1−A−rA'=0 follows rA'=1−A, so dA/(1−A)=dr/r. Claimed equivalence omits a minus sign after integrating: ln(1−A)=-ln r+C.","confidence":0.92}

verdict.explanation: From 1−A−rA'=0 follows rA'=1−A, so dA/(1−A)=dr/r. Claimed equivalence omits a minus sign after integrating: ln(1−A)=-ln r+C.

claim_text: Direct integration produces -ln|1-A| = ln r + C₁, implying 1 - A = C/r.
A(r) = 1 - C/r, where C > 0 is an integration constant.
raw_response: {"claim_id":22,"verdict":"UNJUSTIFIED","explanation":"Correct for vacuum Schwarzschild after solving full Einstein equations and asymptotic flatness, but this statement alone lacks derivation and justification for sign/positivity of C.","confidence":0.94}

verdict.explanation: Correct for vacuum Schwarzschild after solving full Einstein equations and asymptotic flatness, but this statement alone lacks derivation and justification for sign/positivity of C.

claim_text: The sign C > 0 ensures a sensible Newtonian limit.
raw_response: {"claim_id": 23, "verdict": "CONFIRMED", "explanation": "A positive C (set as 2GM/c²) yields g_tt ~ 1 - 2GM/(rc²), matching the attractive Newtonian potential.", "confidence": 1.0}

verdict.explanation: A positive C (set as 2GM/c²) yields g_tt ~ 1 - 2GM/(rc²), matching the attractive Newtonian potential.

claim_text: The Schwarzschild metric is ds² = -(1 - C/r)c² dt² + dr²/(1 - C/r) + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).
raw_response: {"claim_id":24,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"This is the standard Schwarzschild-form line element in areal radius coordinates, with integration constant C later fixed to 2GM/c^2 by boundary conditions/Newtonian limit.","confidence":0.98}

verdict.explanation: This is the standard Schwarzschild-form line element in areal radius coordinates, with integration constant C later fixed to 2GM/c^2 by boundary conditions/Newtonian limit.

claim_text: The remaining equation R_{tt} = 0 or R_{rr} = 0 is automatically satisfied by this form.

verdict.explanation: Using spherical symmetry and Bianchi identities, one Einstein equation becomes redundant once the others are solved.

claim_text: A(r) ≈ 1 - 2GM/(c²r), implying C = 2GM/c².
raw_response: {"claim_id":28,"verdict":"INCOMPLETE","explanation":"Correct asymptotic matching gives A(r)=1-C/r with C=2GM/c^2, but only after specifying metric ansatz, vacuum solution, and Newtonian weak-field identification.","confidence":0.95}

verdict.explanation: Correct asymptotic matching gives A(r)=1-C/r with C=2GM/c^2, but only after specifying metric ansatz, vacuum solution, and Newtonian weak-field identification.

claim_text: The Schwarzschild metric is ds² = -(1 - 2GM/(c²r))c² dt² + dr²/(1 - 2GM/(c²r)) + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).

verdict.explanation: Standard Schwarzschild metric; correctly expresses the coordinate singularity at r=2GM/c².

claim_text: A coordinate singularity occurs where the metric coefficients diverge or vanish, i.e., where A(r) = 0 or equivalently where the coefficient of dr² diverges.
raw_response: {"claim_id":30,"verdict":"INCOMPLETE","explanation":"In Schwarzschild coordinates, the horizon is where g_tt=0 and g_rr diverges, but generic metric coefficient zeros/divergences need not be mere coordinate singularities.", "confidence": 0.95}

verdict.explanation: In Schwarzschild coordinates, the horizon is where g_tt=0 and g_rr diverges, but generic metric coefficient zeros/divergences need not be mere coordinate singularities.

claim_text: The coordinate singularity is at r = r_s = 2GM/c².

verdict.explanation: The Schwarzschild metric indeed shows a coordinate singularity at r = 2GM/c².

claim_text: This is a coordinate singularity, not a curvature singularity.
raw_response: {"claim_id":32,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"In Schwarzschild spacetime, r=2GM/c^2 makes metric components singular in Schwarzschild coordinates, but curvature invariants remain finite there; the true curvature singularity is at r=0.","confidence":0.99}

verdict.explanation: In Schwarzschild spacetime, r=2GM/c^2 makes metric components singular in Schwarzschild coordinates, but curvature invariants remain finite there; the true curvature singularity is at r=0.

claim_text: The Schwarzschild radius is r_s = 2GM/c².

verdict.explanation: The standard vacuum Schwarzschild solution gives r_s = 2GM/c², matching the derived result.

claim_text: The R_{tr} equation, after substituting Christoffel symbols, yields ∂_r(A'/A + B'/B) = 0.
raw_response: {"claim_id":36,"verdict":"ERROR","explanation":"For static spherical metric ds²=-A(r)c²dt²+B(r)dr²+r²dΩ², R_tr vanishes identically since no t-dependence; it does not imply ∂r(A'/A+B'/B)=0.","confidence":0.99}

verdict.explanation: For static spherical metric ds²=-A(r)c²dt²+B(r)dr²+r²dΩ², R_tr vanishes identically since no t-dependence; it does not imply ∂r(A'/A+B'/B)=0.

claim_text: The cancellations yielding the compact form of R_{θθ} include csc²θ - cot²θ = 1 from angular Christoffel symbols.

verdict.explanation: The identity csc²θ - cot²θ = 1 is correct and properly cancels angular terms in the Rθθ component.

claim_text: R_{θθ} = 0 holds identically for flat spacetime when A = B = 1.
raw_response: {"claim_id":38,"verdict":"CONFIRMED","explanation":"For ds²=-dt²+dr²+r²dΩ², spherical-coordinate Ricci components vanish. Specifically R_{θθ}=0 despite nonzero Christoffels, confirming flat spacetime.","confidence":0.99}

verdict.explanation: For ds²=-dt²+dr²+r²dΩ², spherical-coordinate Ricci components vanish. Specifically R_{θθ}=0 despite nonzero Christoffels, confirming flat spacetime.
